Caption: Minimalist design of the flight train highlighting the balloon and payload reference systems with representation of their relative movements.
Payload with integrated sensor and cameras for stratospheric Earth monitoring.
Flight train configuration showing the four-section design and dummyTXdummy– the orientation of the two IMUs; X axis in blue, Y in yellow and Z in violet.

Laboratory characterization setup for flight train modal analysis. The upper clamp is held fixed while controlled perturbations are manually applied to the suspended payload structure. Longitudinal stresses (vertical displacement, 5–10 cm) induce axial oscillations; horizontal perturbations (50–100 cm lateral displacement) excite pendular modes; combined multi-directional excitation generates chaotic dynamics including coupled torsional oscillations around the chain vertical axis, simulating the complex forcing environment encountered during stratospheric flight.

Raw data of payload accelerometers (on the left) and rate gyros (on the right) for all the test duration. The ’quiet’ areas correspond to moments when the system is not under stress. This was done to clearly identify the various outputs based on the inputs. In the image, all the three axes are shown.
Response of the XY axes accelerometer to the pendulum input movement on the first direction.
Response of the XY axes accelerometer to the pendulum input movement on the second direction.
Response on the Z axis accelerometer to the longitudinal input oscillation.
Response of XY accelerometer (left) and the Z accelerometer (right) to the coupled and mixed movement imposed in input on the chain.
Left: 3D trajectory from the take-off to the landing. Right: 2D ground track showing 150 km eastward drift due to strong stratospheric winds.
Raw tri-axial accelerometers data (left) and gyroscopes data (right) from the payload Pixhawk unit. The X, Y and Z axes are respectively red, magenta and black.
Zoomed view of the payload accelerometer data during the ascent phase (0-6000 s), showing the detailed dynamic behavior before descent.
Pitch time series for balloon and payload IMUs.
Roll (left) and yaw (right) time series for balloon and payload IMUs.
Relative pitch between balloon and payload versus altitude.
Relative roll between balloon and payload versus altitude.
Relative yaw between balloon and payload versus altitude.
FFT of flight accelerometer data on Z axis. Left: FFT of the balloon accelerometer output. Right: FFT of the relative output (balloon-payload).
Relative pendulum frequencies enhancing the remaining compound pendulum peak.
FFT of flight accelerometer data on XY axes. Left: X-axis spectrum. Right: Y-axis spectrum with dominant peaks highlighted.

On the left is a shot from the sports camera taken a few moments after the balloon was released. On the right is the image captured at the same time by the colour camera with the triple band filter. The footprint is different because the camera used for analysis has a much narrower lens than the fisheye lens of the sports camera.
Image captured by the sports camera shortly before the fall.
From left to right: image of laboratory tests carried out on the flight chain; two images of the moments before launch.
